title: Honeycomb
subtitle: Brittle caramel sponge candy with a crisp, airy texture
excerpt: Golden brittle candy made by cooking sugar and golden syrup to hard crack, then adding bicarbonate of soda which foams the caramel into a honeycomb structure. Shatters like glass. Also called cinder toffee or sponge candy.
sourceNotes: Honeycomb (also known as cinder toffee, hokey pokey, or sponge candy) is a confection made across Britain and Ireland. The technique of foaming a hot sugar syrup with bicarbonate of soda to create a porous, brittle candy is documented in British confectionery guides from the Victorian period. The name honeycomb refers to the visual resemblance to the wax cells of a beehive. The recipe requires a sugar thermometer for accurate results; the hard crack stage is critical.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 1 week. Humidity causes the honeycomb to soften and become sticky; store with a sachet of silica gel if possible.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hard crack is the sugar cooking stage at 150 to 155°C, where the syrup has lost almost all its water. A drop of the syrup in cold water forms a hard, brittle thread that snaps rather than bending. This is the temperature at which bicarbonate of soda is added to make honeycomb: below 150°C, the resulting candy is chewy rather than brittle; above 160°C, the sugar begins to burn and taste bitter.

content.text: Honeycomb is a three-ingredient confection that requires no skill except reading a thermometer. The

at 150°C is the moment to add the bicarbonate of soda. Below that temperature the candy will be chewy; above it the sugar will begin to darken toward burnt toffee. Move fast once the bicarb goes in: the foam only lasts a few seconds.

attrs.prepNote: measured and ready before you start cooking

content.text: Line a baking tray with baking paper. Measure out the bicarbonate of soda and keep it beside the hob in a small bowl with a spoon ready. Place the sugar and golden syrup in a medium saucepan without stirring. Heat over medium heat until the sugar dissolves, then continue to cook without stirring until the mixture reaches 150°C on a sugar thermometer; it will turn a deep amber colour. Remove from the heat immediately and quickly tip in the bicarbonate of soda. Stir once or twice, just enough to mix it through; the mixture will foam dramatically. Pour immediately onto the lined tray. Do not spread it; let it find its own level. Leave undisturbed for at least 30 minutes until completely set and cold. Break into pieces.
